extra.reasoning:
The property globally(opted_out implies not sent) is VIOLATED, reachable within the 12-event bound.

The only transition that can set sent:=true while opted_out is true is [t_resume_glitch]: on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks >= 8): sent := true. This directly creates a state with opted_out=true and sent=true, breaking the invariant. The remaining sent-setting rule [t_send_allowed] requires NOT opted_out, and [t_send_blocked] forces sent:=false under opted_out, so they are safe in isolation.

To enable t_resume_glitch we must reach opted_out=true AND ticks>=8. ticks is raised by the tick event, which increments ticks until it saturates at 8 (and incidentally sets sent:=false). opted_out is set by the optout event.

Witness execution (10 events, within bound):
1-8. tick x8 -> ticks climbs 1..8 (saturated at 8); sent=false; opted_out=false.
9. optout -> opted_out=true, sent=false.
10. send_attempt -> guard (opted_out and ticks>=8) holds, so t_resume_glitch fires, setting sent:=true while opted_out remains true.

Resulting state: opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=8 -> opted_out is true but sent is NOT false, violating the invariant.

Note on rule conflict: on send_attempt with opted_out=true and ticks>=8, both t_send_blocked (guard opted_out) and t_resume_glitch (guard opted_out and ticks>=8) are enabled. The property must hold on EVERY reachable execution, so the nondeterministic choice that selects t_resume_glitch is a valid execution and exhibits the violation; the existence of a safe alternative (t_send_blocked) does not save the property. This is the intended "resume glitch" bug.
